comment_text.sn2.25:1.2: Normally kuṭikā is understood as a diminutive of kuṭi, hence “little hut”. But here several mendicants are staying (as at Ud 4.2:1.3), while elsewhere the Buddha stayed in one while at the Jetavana (DN 14:1.1.2). More likely the suffix here does not change the meaning of kuṭi.
comment_text.sn2.25:2.1: The deity Jantu (“personage”, “man”, “offspring”) cannot be identified.
comment_text.sn2.25:4.4: Monks falling asleep inappropriately is addressed in the Vinaya (Bu Pc 5, Bu Ss 1:2.1.1, Kd 8:16.1.1; see also Kd 1:7.2.2). | Mucchita in Pali usually means “infatuated, besotted”, but the normal meaning of Sanskrit mūrchita is “fainted, passed out”.

root_text.sn2.25:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ sambahulā bhikkhū, kosalesu viharanti himavantapasse araññakuṭikāya uddhatā unnaḷā capalā mukharā vikiṇṇavācā muṭṭhassatino asampajānā asamāhitā vibbhantacittā pākatindriyā.
root_text.sn2.25:2.1: Atha kho jantu devaputto tadahuposathe pannarase yena te bhikkhū tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā te bhikkhū gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:

translation_text.sn2.25:1.2: At one time several mendicants were staying in the Kosalan lands, in a wilderness hut on the slopes of the Himalayas. They were restless, insolent, fickle, scurrilous, loose-tongued, unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying minds and undisciplined faculties.
translation_text.sn2.25:2.1: Then on the fifteenth day sabbath the godling Jantu went up to those mendicants and addressed them in verse:
translation_text.sn2.25:3.5: Knowing that the world was impermanent
translation_text.sn2.25:3.6: they made an end of suffering.
translation_text.sn2.25:4.1: But now they’ve made themselves hard to look after,
translation_text.sn2.25:4.3: They eat and eat and then lie down,
translation_text.sn2.25:4.4: passed out in the homes of others.
translation_text.sn2.25:5.1: Having raised my joined palms to the Saṅgha,
translation_text.sn2.25:5.2: I speak here only about certain people.
translation_text.sn2.25:5.4: just like those who have passed away.
